content:
# Luke Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 119-120

---

**1** At the first light of dawn on the first day of the week, the women came to the tomb carrying the burial spices they had prepared.

**2** They found the stone rolled away from the entrance of the tomb,

**3** but when they went inside, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus.

**4** While they stood there bewildered by this, suddenly two men appeared beside them in blazing garments.

**5** The women were terrified and dropped their faces to the ground. The men said to them, "Why are you searching among the dead for someone who is alive?

**6** He is not here — he has risen! Remember what he told you while he was still in Galilee:

**7** 'The Son of Man must be handed over to sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again.'"

**8** Then they remembered his words.

**9** When they returned from the tomb, they reported all of this to the Eleven and to everyone else.

**10** It was Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the other women with them who told the apostles these things.

**11** But the apostles dismissed their words as nonsense and refused to believe them.

**12** Peter, however, jumped up and ran to the tomb. He bent down and saw only the linen burial wrappings lying there. He went away, stunned at what had happened.

**13** That same day, two of them were walking to a village called Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem,

**14** talking with each other about everything that had taken place.

**15** While they were deep in conversation and discussion, Jesus himself came alongside them and began walking with them,

**16** but their eyes were kept from recognizing him.

**17** He asked them, "What are you discussing so intently with each other as you walk?" They stopped in their tracks, their faces heavy with grief.

**18** One of them, named Cleopas, answered him, "Are you the only visitor to Jerusalem who doesn't know what has happened there these past few days?"

**19** "What things?" he asked. They said to him, "About Jesus of Nazareth — a man who was a prophet mighty in action and word before God and all the people.

**20** Our chief priests and leaders handed him over to be sentenced to death, and they crucified him.

**21** But we had hoped that he was the one who would redeem Israel. And on top of all this, today is the third day since it happened.

**22** Then some women from our group shocked us — they went to the tomb early this morning

**23** and did not find his body. They came back saying they had seen a vision of angels who told them he is alive!

**24** Some of our companions went to the tomb and found it exactly as the women had described, but him they did not see."

**25** Then he said to them, "How slow your hearts are to trust everything the prophets declared!

**26** Was it not necessary for the Messiah to suffer these things and then enter into his glory?"

**27** And beginning with Moses and working through all the Prophets, he explained to them what was written about himself throughout the entire Scriptures.

**28** As they approached the village where they were headed, he acted as though he would continue farther.

**29** But they pressed him urgently, saying, "Stay with us — it is nearly evening and the day is almost gone." So he went in to stay with them.

**30** When he had taken his place at the table with them, he took the bread, blessed it, broke it, and gave it to them.

**31** At that instant their eyes were opened, and they recognized him — and he vanished from their sight.

**32** They said to each other, "Weren't our hearts burning inside us while he was talking with us on the road, while he was opening the Scriptures to us?"

**33** They got up immediately and returned to Jerusalem. There they found the Eleven and their companions gathered together,

**34** who told them, "It's true! The Lord has risen — he has appeared to Simon!"

**35** Then the two described what had happened on the road, and how they had recognized him in the breaking of the bread.

**36** While they were still speaking about this, Jesus himself stood among them and said to them, "Peace be with you."

**37** They were startled and terrified, thinking they were seeing a ghost.

**38** He said to them, "Why are you so shaken? Why do doubts flood your hearts?

**39** Look at my hands and my feet — it is really me. Touch me and see for yourselves. A ghost does not have flesh and bones as you can see I have."

**40** And saying this, he showed them his hands and his feet.

**41** While they still could not believe it because of sheer joy and astonishment, he said to them, "Do you have anything here to eat?"

**42** They gave him a piece of broiled fish,

**43** and he took it and ate it right in front of them.

**44** Then he said to them, "This is what I told you while I was still with you: everything written about me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms must be fulfilled."

**45** Then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures.

**46** He told them, "This is what stands written: the Messiah will suffer and rise from the dead on the third day,

**47** and in his name, repentance for the forgiveness of sins will be proclaimed to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem.

**48** You are witnesses of these things.

**49** And look — I am sending upon you what my Father promised. But stay in the city until you have been clothed with power from on high."

**50** Then he led them out as far as Bethany, and lifting his hands, he blessed them.

**51** While he was blessing them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven.

**52** They worshiped him and returned to Jerusalem overflowing with joy.

**53** And they were continually in the temple, praising God.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "At the first light of dawn":** The Greek (ορθρου βαθεως) literally reads "deep dawn" — an idiom for the earliest possible moment of daylight. "First light of dawn" captures the urgency and the liminal darkness-to-light moment, which is thematically resonant with what the women are about to discover.

**Verse 4 — "blazing garments":** The Sinaiticus reads εσθησεσιν αστραπτουσαις — garments that flash like lightning. "Dazzling" is the common rendering, but "blazing" better conveys the visceral shock the women experience.

**Verse 5 — "Why are you searching among the dead for someone who is alive?":** This is one of the most powerful lines in the New Testament. The Greek (τι ζητειτε τον ζωντα μετα των νεκρων) sets up a stark contrast: the living one (τον ζωντα) among the dead (των νεκρων). I have rendered this as a pointed question that carries the full force of the rebuke — the search itself is the problem.

**Verse 11 — "nonsense":** The Greek ληρος means idle talk, babble, medical delirium. "Nonsense" captures the dismissiveness the apostles felt — they did not merely doubt; they actively disbelieved.

**Verse 12 — "stunned":** The Greek θαυμαζων has a range from "wondering" to "marveling." In context, Peter has not yet come to faith — he is bewildered. "Stunned" captures the state between confusion and emerging realization.

**Verse 17 — "stopped in their tracks, their faces heavy with grief":** The Greek εσταθησαν σκυθρωποι carries both the physical halt and the emotional weight. σκυθρωποι means gloomy-faced, sullen with sorrow. I combined these into a single image of people arrested by their own grief.

**Verse 25 — "How slow your hearts are to trust":** The Greek ανοητοι (literally "mindless, foolish") and βραδεις τη καρδια ("slow of heart") convey not intellectual failure but a failure of trust. I chose "slow hearts" and "trust" over "foolish" and "believe" because the rebuke is about their inability to let the prophetic word reshape their expectations.

**Verse 29 — "Stay with us":** The verb μεινον (remain, abide) carries deep resonance in Luke's theology. The urgency of the invitation — pressing him to stay — is a quiet turning point. They are unknowingly inviting God to their table.

**Verse 31 — "he vanished from their sight":** The Greek αυτος αφαντος εγενετο απ αυτων — literally "he became invisible from them." This is not departure but disappearance. The recognition and the vanishing happen in the same breath, emphasizing that presence is no longer tied to physical visibility.

**Verse 32 — "burning inside us":** The Greek καιομενη (burning) is vivid and somatic. This is not a metaphor they would have chosen beforehand — it is recognized only in hindsight. The road to Emmaus is a story about how presence precedes recognition.

**Verse 44 — "the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms":** This is the threefold division of the Hebrew Bible (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim — with Psalms standing as representative of the Writings). Jesus claims the entire scriptural tradition as testimony about himself.

**Verse 49 — "clothed with power from on high":** The Greek ενδυσησθε (to be clothed, to put on) uses a garment metaphor for the coming of the Spirit. Power is not merely received — it is worn. This points forward to Pentecost (Acts 2).

**Verse 51 — Ascension:** Sinaiticus includes και ανεφερετο εις τον ουρανον ("and was carried up into heaven"). Some manuscripts omit this clause, but Sinaiticus preserves it. Luke's Gospel thus ends as it began — in the temple, with worship, under an open heaven.

**Verse 53 — "praising God":** The Sinaiticus reads αινουντες τον θεον. Some manuscripts add "Amen" at the close. Luke's final image is a community gathered in continuous praise — the grief of the crucifixion has been transmuted entirely into uncontainable joy.

**Structural note on Luke 24:** This chapter operates as a single sustained day — from dawn at the tomb to evening at Emmaus to night in Jerusalem. Luke compresses the entire Resurrection experience into one breathless day, creating a literary arc from absence to presence, from grief to joy, from blindness to sight. Every encounter follows the same pattern: Jesus is present but unrecognized, then he opens their understanding through Scripture and bread, and recognition breaks through. The chapter is Luke's theology of how the risen Christ will continue to be known — through the word and the meal.

— PNEUMA
